EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After weeks of unseasonably warm weather across south-central Nebraska, a strong arctic surge brought a quick end to the warmth.  Temperatures steadily declined into the single digits by the evening of the 20th as arctic air sagged south across the area.  Snowfall for the event was light and ranged from 1 to 3 inches.  As the cold air deepened by the morning of the 22nd, temperatures fell well below zero.  The coldest morning of the season so far was on the 23rd, when temperatures dropped as low as 17 below zero with clear skies.  Wind chill readings ranged from 20 to 45 below zero.  The cold surge gave way to warmer weather by Christmas Day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds took the roof off of a 150 ft machine shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure which tracked across eastern Nebraska brought a long period of very strong west to northwest winds to the area.  The barometric pressure from this storm dropped to 28.86 inches at Omaha Eppley-Airfield at 225 am cst...the 5th lowest on record.  Wind gusts of 45 mph to around 65 mph were common over all of eastern Nebraska from around 200 am cst through 400 pm cst before subsiding.  The high winds toppled a few weaker trees, downed or snapped some power poles in rural areas, overturned several semi-tractor trailer trucks and did isolated sign damage.   Peak wind gusts and time of occurrence included...66 mph at the Valley NWS office at 1110 am...63 mph at Fremont at 210 pm...61 mph at Tekamah at 530 am...58 mph at Offutt AFB at 334 pm...55 mph at Omaha Eppley-Airfield at 112 pm...55 mph at Columbus at 534 am...54 mph at Beatrice at 225 pm...52 mph at Falls City at 1152 am and 1252 pm and 48 mph at Lincoln at 220 am and 1244 pm.

Icy roads caused accidents across Kimball County.  On the 19th, a motorinst lost control of his pickup and rolled it, 2 miles north of Kimball.  There were no injuries, but the vehicle was totaled.  On the 20th, a driver of a car eastbond on Interstate 80, hit a dump truck which was also eastbound.  Two children in the car received minor injuries.  Another accident occurred 4 miles east of Kimball in which a car rear ended a dump truck.  No injuries occurred in this accident.

A powerful winter storm passed through northern Nebraska November 9 and 10. Although snowfall amounts were generally less than 4 inches, strong wind made conditions much worse than they would be otherwise. In north central Nebraska between Valentine and O'Neill, severe wind caused widespread power outages . In O'Neill the wind gusted to 62 mph. Winds higher than 50 mph were common. In other parts of the area, the storm resulted in numerous traffic accidents and widespread closings of schools and businesses.

In north central Nebraska between Valentine and O'Neill, severe wind caused widespread power outages . In O'Neill the wind gusted to 62 mph. Winds higher than 50 mph were common. In other parts of the area, the storm resulted in numerous traffic accidents and widespread closings of schools and businesses.

An unusual October severe weather outbreak spawned a tornado which tore across parts of Phelps and western Buffalo counties.  The tornado set down a few miles southwest of Loomis and rapidly moved northeast.  Along its path in Phelps county, grain bins were damaged, power poles snapped and a roof was torn from a large storage building.  One farmstead northeast of Loomis sustained major damage.  The roof was ripped from the house and two walls were moved from the foundation.  A pole barn was destroyed.  Both the house and barn were a total loss.  Once the storm crossed the Platte River, the tornado hit a trailer house just west of Elm Creek and deposited the shattered remains about 200 feet north of its original location.  The tornado continued to move north and shortly dissipated.Another tornado formed from a different storm in northern Dawson county.  This tornado passed through a farmstead causing tree damage, blew over grain bins, and damaged other buildings.  The house sustained only minor damage.

A three mile long ice jam formed along the Platte river to the southwest of Fremont upstream from the highway 77 bridge to Big Island.  The jam caused water to rise upstream and resulted in lowland flooding at Ridgeland Road, Hormel Park, the YMCA Camp, and Big Island.  In addition, 8 homes along the Platte river were flooded receiving 1 to 4 inches of water in the main level.

An ice jam along the Platte River in the southwest part of Hall county forced the relocation of one family from their home.  The jam had been in place for days and water flow remained restricted.  Sand bags were used to divert the flow of water from the home for a time.  The jam broke loose on the 21st allowing the family to return home safely.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure brought locally heavy snow to northeast Nebraska accompanied by winds gusting to near  60 mph,  see the entry for  the same time period for the rest of eastern Nebraska.  Heavier snowfall totals included 8 inches at Laurel, 6 inches at Loretto and 4 inches at Norfolk, Bloomfield, Osmond and Hadar.  The snow was whipped around by northwest winds of 30 to over 50 mph which prevailed from around 200 am cst through 400 pm cst before subsiding.  The strong winds created near whiteout conditions at times and closed many schools.  Power was lost for a while at many locations due to the strong wind and ice accumulations on power lines or broken tree limbs falling on power lines.   A peak gust of 58 mph was recorded at Norfolk at 727 am cst with similar gusts across all of northeast Nebraska.  Even higher gusts were reported across parts of east central Nebraska.

Severe Thunderstorms ripped across south-central Nebraska dropping large hail, very heavy rains and producing wind gusts to 70 mph.  South of Ravenna, several hog sheds on a farmstead were destroyed by high winds.  Corn and soybean crops in the area were heavily damaged.  The swath of destructive hail moved northeast across northwest Howard, northeast Sherman, southeast Valley and the southern half of Greeley counties.  In this area, over 400,000 acres of alfalfa, corn and soybeans were damaged.  Up to 70 percent damage was reported in Greeley county.  Near Elba, hail and wind broke windows and damaged homes.  In addition to the hail and wind, heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches was reported near Scotia.  Widespread 1 to 4 inch rains were reported elsewhere.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs down plus some damage to crops and buildings around Humphrey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record cold early morning temperatures with lows near or slightly below freezing in most locations.  Tender crops and garden suffered unknown damage.  It was the coldest for so late in the season at a few locations.

A thin layer of ice caused a rollover accident on highway 385 a quarter of a mile south of the Box Butte county line.  The 39 year old driver of the car lost control on the ice and drove into the east ditch and rolled the vehicle.  The driver and a passenger were both ejected.  The driver sustained a broken collar bone and the passenger had a severe leg cut.  The vehicle was totalled.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed trees.  One trees feel onto a pickup truck causing significant damage.  A center pivot irrigation system was damaged two miles south of Western.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms roared across south-central Nebraska just after midnight CDT downing power lines, damaging buildings and over turning irrigation center pivots.  The storms slipped southeast through Bertrand at 12:45 am CDT knocking power out to a portion of the town.  As the thunderstorms moved southeast, they intensified and eventually produced 60 to 80 mph winds in a 20 mile wide swath from 20 miles southwest of Hastings to 30 miles southeast of York.The damage began near Bladen as 20 pivots were upset in northern Webster county.  The most extensive damage was reported from southern Clay and northern Thayer counties.  In Glenvil, large trees were uprooted and grain bins blown apart.  At Carlton, eight large grain bins were destroyed and a grain dryer overturned.  In Davenport, windows were blown out of homes by the wind.  Some homes and most out buildings in the high wind corridor sustained minor damage as well.  In all it was estimated 75 to 100 center pivots were overturned by the wind.  Portions of northern Thayer county were without power for 12 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One home lost a roof.  Grain bins were blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unusual post-Memorial Day frost and freeze left its mark on crops across a small part of south-central Nebraska.  The most concentrated area of damaged fields stretched along the Republican River from Red Cloud to Cambridge.  Nearly every community along the river reported overnight low temperatures for the 4th and 5th near 32 degrees.  Low lying areas near the river's edge dropped into the upper 20s under the clear sky.  The result was damaged leaves on several thousand acres of corn and soybeans.  Fortunately, in most cases, the growing point of the plants was not damaged enough to stop plant growth.  After the frost, warmer and wet weather provided nearly ideal conditions for the crops to rebound to normal with very little, if any, yield loss expected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbling across south-central Nebraska early in the day were responsible for dropping large hail and heavy rain in parts of the area.  Hail the size of baseballs was reported.  Windshields were shattered in the Lexington area and torrential rainfall forced water over US Highway 30 for a short time west of Kearney.  The heavy rains caused the roof over the Kearney/Buffalo County law enforcement building to collapse.  Damage to the 911 center was estimated to be near $400,000.  Street flooding was common in the rest of Kearney.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed.  Power outages.
Wind damage across much of Wayne county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorm activity across southern Nebraska dumped heavy rain, hail, and produced strong winds.  Wind gusts from 55 to 80 mph were common throughout the area with some tree damage northwest of Stromsburg, near Scotia, and six inch limbs broken in Trumbull.  Hail to 1.75 inch diameter resulted in sporadic, but minor, crop damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down.
Thunderstorms produced strong winds which tipped over a grain bin and did damage to a home and shed.
A tornado was on the ground for approximately 1 mile doing minor damage to two center pivot irrigation systems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across south-central Nebraska in the evening and early morning hours.  The first dumped hail as large as soft balls on Holdrege and the surrounding area.  Dozens of cars and trucks were damaged or totaled by the large hail.  A fertilizer operation southwest of town sustained major damage to a storage bin as the hail went through the steel roof.  Roofs and siding was damaged along with windows broken and blown in.  The first round of thunderstorms also spawned two brief tornado touchdowns to the west of Minden.  Both were reported by trained spotters in the area.  Large hail north of Arapahoe severely damaged the wheat crop in the area.The second round of storms rolled through around midnight CDT with torrential rains and wind driven hail.  Damage was generally confined to crops and, in particular, across a part of northern Hamilton county.  As many as fifty sections of beans, corn, and alfalfa were damaged by the wind and hail.  Nine sections sustained major damage.  Throughout the area, widespread one to four inch rains posed problems for low lying areas and waterlogged early season crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwest winds gusting over 60 mph belted south-central Nebraska for most of the day.  Sustained wind speeds from 35 to 45 mph were common all day long with gusts over 60 mph reported.  Ord reported a wind gusts of 67 mph for the highest gust in the area.  Aurora and Grand Island reported wind gusts to 61 and 60 mph respectively.  Numerous other cities reported wind gusts to 55 mph.  Fortunately, no significant damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting north and a strong jet stream played a key role in the eruption of severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon in south-central Nebraska.  Large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds and two tornadic thunderstorms caused millions of dollars in damage.The first tornadic thunderstorm occurred in northern Gosper county just outside of the town of Elwood.  The tornado sat down to the southwest of Elwood then travelled east.  After a mile, the tornado turned and travelled due north.  It hit one farmstead, heavily damaging the home and totally destroying several outbuildings.  The path of the tornado when right down the middle of US highway 283.  A truck driver and a family of three were travelling north of Elwood when they realized the tornado was heading toward them.  They abandon their vehicles and sought cover in a ditch.  The tornado passed nearly overhead but the four survived with only minor injuries.  The one car was totalled and the semi-truck, loaded with ice cream, was heavily damaged.  The cargo was a total loss.  This same severe thunderstorm marched northeast and dropped another brief tornado just southeast of Odessa but no significant damage was reported.Another severe thunderstorm, which began in Smith county, Kansas, moved just north of the stateline and produced at least two confirmed touchdowns.  One was reported two miles north of Bostwick but did little damage.  Another tornado passed just to the southeast of Chester and damaged a farmstead.  The tornado sat down about a mile east of town then travelled southeast.  Damage at the farm was mainly confined to a couple of outbuildings.  The people in the farm house were not injured.  At least 25 center irrigation pivots were lost to the storm.  Damaging baseball sized hail was noted in Nelson and near Red Cloud.  Hail to the size of soft balls was reported eight miles southeast of Red Cloud.  In Clay county, residents were nailed twice with strong winds and hail during the evening.  The first occurred near 6 pm as 60 to 80 mph winds roared across the county.  The winds were likely outflow from the severe thunderstorm just to the south of the area.  At least two farmsteads had out building damage and tree damage was significant.  About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After weeks of unseasonably warm weather across south-central Nebraska, a strong arctic surge brought a quick end to the warmth.  Temperatures steadily declined into the single digits by the evening of the 20th as arctic air sagged south across the area.  Snowfall for the event was light and ranged from 1 to 3 inches.  As the cold air deepened by the morning of the 22nd, temperatures fell well below zero.  The coldest morning of the season so far was on the 23rd, when temperatures dropped as low as 17 below zero with clear skies.  Wind chill readings ranged from 20 to 45 below zero.  The cold surge gave way to warmer weather by Christmas Day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Playground equipment destroyed at park.
Lightning struck a home causing severe damage.
Power poles blown down.
Basements half full.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across south-central Nebraska in the evening and early morning hours.  The first dumped hail as large as soft balls on Holdrege and the surrounding area.  Dozens of cars and trucks were damaged or totaled by the large hail.  A fertilizer operation southwest of town sustained major damage to a storage bin as the hail went through the steel roof.  Roofs and siding was damaged along with windows broken and blown in.  The first round of thunderstorms also spawned two brief tornado touchdowns to the west of Minden.  Both were reported by trained spotters in the area.  Large hail north of Arapahoe severely damaged the wheat crop in the area.The second round of storms rolled through around midnight CDT with torrential rains and wind driven hail.  Damage was generally confined to crops and, in particular, across a part of northern Hamilton county.  As many as fifty sections of beans, corn, and alfalfa were damaged by the wind and hail.  Nine sections sustained major damage.  Throughout the area, widespread one to four inch rains posed problems for low lying areas and waterlogged early season crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbling across south-central Nebraska early in the day were responsible for dropping large hail and heavy rain in parts of the area.  Hail the size of baseballs was reported.  Windshields were shattered in the Lexington area and torrential rainfall forced water over US Highway 30 for a short time west of Kearney.  The heavy rains caused the roof over the Kearney/Buffalo County law enforcement building to collapse.  Damage to the 911 center was estimated to be near $400,000.  Street flooding was common in the rest of Kearney.

Late evening severe thunderstorms rolled across the northeast half of south-central Nebraska dropping hail and heavy rains with their passage.  The thunderstorms moved southeast across Howard and Merrick counties near 10 pm and dumped hail the size of softballs near St. Paul and Palmer.  Windshields were damaged and windows knocked out of homes near Palmer.  Later, thunderstorms in Clay county dumped large hail and overturned a pivot system southeast of Saronville.  Significant crop damage was reported in southeast Valley county and across Howard and Merrick counties.  Much of the alfalfa crop was leveled as was much of the corn and soybeans in the path of the thunderstorms.  Many farmers replanted their crops.  In some places, drifts of hail were still in fields and lawns 24 hours after the thunderstorms passed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Semi truck blown over.
Tree limbs downed.
Garage destroyed.  Machine shed destroyed.
Reported in rural areas near the town of Seward.
Thunderstorms produced up to 6 inches of rain in a short time causing some flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Midday thunderstorms spawned a tornado south of York damaging a farmstead significantly.  The tornado set down two miles north of Fairmont and was on the ground for seven miles before lifting just east of McCool Junction.  The tornado nearly destroyed at least one farmstead and caused damage to power poles and lines and center pivot systems.  A semi tractor trailer was overturned three miles southeast of York.  The driver sustained minor injuries.  The same storm also produced a brief tornado south of Benedict destroying two hog confinement facilities but no hogs were injured.

Severe Thunderstorms ripped across south-central Nebraska dropping large hail, very heavy rains and producing wind gusts to 70 mph.  South of Ravenna, several hog sheds on a farmstead were destroyed by high winds.  Corn and soybean crops in the area were heavily damaged.  The swath of destructive hail moved northeast across northwest Howard, northeast Sherman, southeast Valley and the southern half of Greeley counties.  In this area, over 400,000 acres of alfalfa, corn and soybeans were damaged.  Up to 70 percent damage was reported in Greeley county.  Near Elba, hail and wind broke windows and damaged homes.  In addition to the hail and wind, heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches was reported near Scotia.  Widespread 1 to 4 inch rains were reported elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A three mile long ice jam continued along the Platte river to the southwest of Fremont upstream from the highway 77 bridge to Big Island.  The jam was in the process of breaking up and the water gradually receeded during the first part of February.  The jam resulted in lowland flooding at Ridgeland Road, the YMCA Camp, and Big Island.  Also 8 homes along the Platte river were flooded receiving 1 to 4 inches of water in the main level.  Most of the flooding occurred in January and the water receded from the homes during the first few days of February.

Severe Thunderstorms rumbled across much of south-central Nebraska through the evening hours.  A brief tornado passed northwest of Gilead damaging a shed, irrigation pipe and some fence.  The majority of reports were large hail.  The hail knocked out a windshield at Waco while thunderstorms covered the ground with large hail just south of Gilead.  Given the time of year, crop damage was held to a minimum.  Property damage was also minor.

Severe thunderstorms roared across south-central Nebraska just after midnight CDT downing power lines, damaging buildings and over turning irrigation center pivots.  The storms slipped southeast through Bertrand at 12:45 am CDT knocking power out to a portion of the town.  As the thunderstorms moved southeast, they intensified and eventually produced 60 to 80 mph winds in a 20 mile wide swath from 20 miles southwest of Hastings to 30 miles southeast of York.The damage began near Bladen as 20 pivots were upset in northern Webster county.  The most extensive damage was reported from southern Clay and northern Thayer counties.  In Glenvil, large trees were uprooted and grain bins blown apart.  At Carlton, eight large grain bins were destroyed and a grain dryer overturned.  In Davenport, windows were blown out of homes by the wind.  Some homes and most out buildings in the high wind corridor sustained minor damage as well.  In all it was estimated 75 to 100 center pivots were overturned by the wind.  Portions of northern Thayer county were without power for 12 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball size hail destroyed 160 acres of wheat and broke out windows in a house.

A winter storm produced freezing drizzle and snow which caused icy streets and highways across western and northcentral Nebraska.  A few traffic accidents due to the icy conditions were reported.

Severe Thunderstorms ripped across south-central Nebraska dropping large hail, very heavy rains and producing wind gusts to 70 mph.  South of Ravenna, several hog sheds on a farmstead were destroyed by high winds.  Corn and soybean crops in the area were heavily damaged.  The swath of destructive hail moved northeast across northwest Howard, northeast Sherman, southeast Valley and the southern half of Greeley counties.  In this area, over 400,000 acres of alfalfa, corn and soybeans were damaged.  Up to 70 percent damage was reported in Greeley county.  Near Elba, hail and wind broke windows and damaged homes.  In addition to the hail and wind, heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches was reported near Scotia.  Widespread 1 to 4 inch rains were reported elsewhere.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from thunderstorms caused flooding which damaged some homes on the east side of Ceresco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbling across south-central Nebraska early in the day were responsible for dropping large hail and heavy rain in parts of the area.  Hail the size of baseballs was reported.  Windshields were shattered in the Lexington area and torrential rainfall forced water over US Highway 30 for a short time west of Kearney.  The heavy rains caused the roof over the Kearney/Buffalo County law enforcement building to collapse.  Damage to the 911 center was estimated to be near $400,000.  Street flooding was common in the rest of Kearney.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail at 106th and Brown.
3 farmsteads heavily damaged.

Late evening severe thunderstorms rolled across the northeast half of south-central Nebraska dropping hail and heavy rains with their passage.  The thunderstorms moved southeast across Howard and Merrick counties near 10 pm and dumped hail the size of softballs near St. Paul and Palmer.  Windshields were damaged and windows knocked out of homes near Palmer.  Later, thunderstorms in Clay county dumped large hail and overturned a pivot system southeast of Saronville.  Significant crop damage was reported in southeast Valley county and across Howard and Merrick counties.  Much of the alfalfa crop was leveled as was much of the corn and soybeans in the path of the thunderstorms.  Many farmers replanted their crops.  In some places, drifts of hail were still in fields and lawns 24 hours after the thunderstorms passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across south-central Nebraska in the evening and early morning hours.  The first dumped hail as large as soft balls on Holdrege and the surrounding area.  Dozens of cars and trucks were damaged or totaled by the large hail.  A fertilizer operation southwest of town sustained major damage to a storage bin as the hail went through the steel roof.  Roofs and siding was damaged along with windows broken and blown in.  The first round of thunderstorms also spawned two brief tornado touchdowns to the west of Minden.  Both were reported by trained spotters in the area.  Large hail north of Arapahoe severely damaged the wheat crop in the area.The second round of storms rolled through around midnight CDT with torrential rains and wind driven hail.  Damage was generally confined to crops and, in particular, across a part of northern Hamilton county.  As many as fifty sections of beans, corn, and alfalfa were damaged by the wind and hail.  Nine sections sustained major damage.  Throughout the area, widespread one to four inch rains posed problems for low lying areas and waterlogged early season crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado over open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting north and a strong jet stream played a key role in the eruption of severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon in south-central Nebraska.  Large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds and two tornadic thunderstorms caused millions of dollars in damage.The first tornadic thunderstorm occurred in northern Gosper county just outside of the town of Elwood.  The tornado sat down to the southwest of Elwood then travelled east.  After a mile, the tornado turned and travelled due north.  It hit one farmstead, heavily damaging the home and totally destroying several outbuildings.  The path of the tornado when right down the middle of US highway 283.  A truck driver and a family of three were travelling north of Elwood when they realized the tornado was heading toward them.  They abandon their vehicles and sought cover in a ditch.  The tornado passed nearly overhead but the four survived with only minor injuries.  The one car was totalled and the semi-truck, loaded with ice cream, was heavily damaged.  The cargo was a total loss.  This same severe thunderstorm marched northeast and dropped another brief tornado just southeast of Odessa but no significant damage was reported.Another severe thunderstorm, which began in Smith county, Kansas, moved just north of the stateline and produced at least two confirmed touchdowns.  One was reported two miles north of Bostwick but did little damage.  Another tornado passed just to the southeast of Chester and damaged a farmstead.  The tornado sat down about a mile east of town then travelled southeast.  Damage at the farm was mainly confined to a couple of outbuildings.  The people in the farm house were not injured.  At least 25 center irrigation pivots were lost to the storm.  Damaging baseball sized hail was noted in Nelson and near Red Cloud.  Hail to the size of soft balls was reported eight miles southeast of Red Cloud.  In Clay county, residents were nailed twice with strong winds and hail during the evening.  The first occurred near 6 pm as 60 to 80 mph winds roared across the county.  The winds were likely outflow from the severe thunderstorm just to the south of the area.  At least two farmsteads had out building damage and tree damage was significant.  About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage reported at Millard High School

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across south-central Nebraska in the evening and early morning hours.  The first dumped hail as large as soft balls on Holdrege and the surrounding area.  Dozens of cars and trucks were damaged or totaled by the large hail.  A fertilizer operation southwest of town sustained major damage to a storage bin as the hail went through the steel roof.  Roofs and siding was damaged along with windows broken and blown in.  The first round of thunderstorms also spawned two brief tornado touchdowns to the west of Minden.  Both were reported by trained spotters in the area.  Large hail north of Arapahoe severely damaged the wheat crop in the area.The second round of storms rolled through around midnight CDT with torrential rains and wind driven hail.  Damage was generally confined to crops and, in particular, across a part of northern Hamilton county.  As many as fifty sections of beans, corn, and alfalfa were damaged by the wind and hail.  Nine sections sustained major damage.  Throughout the area, widespread one to four inch rains posed problems for low lying areas and waterlogged early season crops.

Given the time of year, crop damage was held to a minimum.  Property damage was also minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After weeks of unseasonably warm weather across south-central Nebraska, a strong arctic surge brought a quick end to the warmth.  Temperatures steadily declined into the single digits by the evening of the 20th as arctic air sagged south across the area.  Snowfall for the event was light and ranged from 1 to 3 inches.  As the cold air deepened by the morning of the 22nd, temperatures fell well below zero.  The coldest morning of the season so far was on the 23rd, when temperatures dropped as low as 17 below zero with clear skies.  Wind chill readings ranged from 20 to 45 below zero.  The cold surge gave way to warmer weather by Christmas Day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail damaged crops and vehicles as well as houses and other property.

Severe thunderstorms roared across south-central Nebraska just after midnight CDT downing power lines, damaging buildings and over turning irrigation center pivots.  The storms slipped southeast through Bertrand at 12:45 am CDT knocking power out to a portion of the town.  As the thunderstorms moved southeast, they intensified and eventually produced 60 to 80 mph winds in a 20 mile wide swath from 20 miles southwest of Hastings to 30 miles southeast of York.The damage began near Bladen as 20 pivots were upset in northern Webster county.  The most extensive damage was reported from southern Clay and northern Thayer counties.  In Glenvil, large trees were uprooted and grain bins blown apart.  At Carlton, eight large grain bins were destroyed and a grain dryer overturned.  In Davenport, windows were blown out of homes by the wind.  Some homes and most out buildings in the high wind corridor sustained minor damage as well.  In all it was estimated 75 to 100 center pivots were overturned by the wind.  Portions of northern Thayer county were without power for 12 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows broken
Semi truck overturned.  7000 square foot pole barn destroyed west of Syracuse.  Hay sheds flattened.  Most damage confined to an area between Douglas/Syracuse/and Unadilla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record cold early morning temperatures with lows near or slightly below freezing in most locations.  Tender crops and garden suffered unknown damage.  It was the coldest for so late in the season at a few locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2 to 4 inches of rain over Banner County, which resulted in small stream flooding and water flowing over county roads.  The flood waters also damaged or destroyed sunflower, millet, corn, dry bean and wheat crops.
A thunderstorm produced golfball size hail, 5 miles north of Kimball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late evening severe thunderstorms rolled across the northeast half of south-central Nebraska dropping hail and heavy rains with their passage.  The thunderstorms moved southeast across Howard and Merrick counties near 10 pm and dumped hail the size of softballs near St. Paul and Palmer.  Windshields were damaged and windows knocked out of homes near Palmer.  Later, thunderstorms in Clay county dumped large hail and overturned a pivot system southeast of Saronville.  Significant crop damage was reported in southeast Valley county and across Howard and Merrick counties.  Much of the alfalfa crop was leveled as was much of the corn and soybeans in the path of the thunderstorms.  Many farmers replanted their crops.  In some places, drifts of hail were still in fields and lawns 24 hours after the thunderstorms passed.

Thunderstorms rumbling across south-central Nebraska early in the day were responsible for dropping large hail and heavy rain in parts of the area.  Hail the size of baseballs was reported.  Windshields were shattered in the Lexington area and torrential rainfall forced water over US Highway 30 for a short time west of Kearney.  The heavy rains caused the roof over the Kearney/Buffalo County law enforcement building to collapse.  Damage to the 911 center was estimated to be near $400,000.  Street flooding was common in the rest of Kearney.

A warm front lifting north and a strong jet stream played a key role in the eruption of severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon in south-central Nebraska.  Large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds and two tornadic thunderstorms caused millions of dollars in damage.The first tornadic thunderstorm occurred in northern Gosper county just outside of the town of Elwood.  The tornado sat down to the southwest of Elwood then travelled east.  After a mile, the tornado turned and travelled due north.  It hit one farmstead, heavily damaging the home and totally destroying several outbuildings.  The path of the tornado when right down the middle of US highway 283.  A truck driver and a family of three were travelling north of Elwood when they realized the tornado was heading toward them.  They abandon their vehicles and sought cover in a ditch.  The tornado passed nearly overhead but the four survived with only minor injuries.  The one car was totalled and the semi-truck, loaded with ice cream, was heavily damaged.  The cargo was a total loss.  This same severe thunderstorm marched northeast and dropped another brief tornado just southeast of Odessa but no significant damage was reported.Another severe thunderstorm, which began in Smith county, Kansas, moved just north of the stateline and produced at least two confirmed touchdowns.  One was reported two miles north of Bostwick but did little damage.  Another tornado passed just to the southeast of Chester and damaged a farmstead.  The tornado sat down about a mile east of town then travelled southeast.  Damage at the farm was mainly confined to a couple of outbuildings.  The people in the farm house were not injured.  At least 25 center irrigation pivots were lost to the storm.  Damaging baseball sized hail was noted in Nelson and near Red Cloud.  Hail to the size of soft balls was reported eight miles southeast of Red Cloud.  In Clay county, residents were nailed twice with strong winds and hail during the evening.  The first occurred near 6 pm as 60 to 80 mph winds roared across the county.  The winds were likely outflow from the severe thunderstorm just to the south of the area.  At least two farmsteads had out building damage and tree damage was significant.  About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

At least two farmsteads had out building damage and tree damage was significant.  About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

A warm front lifting north and a strong jet stream played a key role in the eruption of severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon in south-central Nebraska.  Large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds and two tornadic thunderstorms caused millions of dollars in damage.The first tornadic thunderstorm occurred in northern Gosper county just outside of the town of Elwood.  The tornado sat down to the southwest of Elwood then travelled east.  After a mile, the tornado turned and travelled due north.  It hit one farmstead, heavily damaging the home and totally destroying several outbuildings.  The path of the tornado when right down the middle of US highway 283.  A truck driver and a family of three were travelling north of Elwood when they realized the tornado was heading toward them.  They abandon their vehicles and sought cover in a ditch.  The tornado passed nearly overhead but the four survived with only minor injuries.  The one car was totalled and the semi-truck, loaded with ice cream, was heavily damaged.  The cargo was a total loss.  This same severe thunderstorm marched northeast and dropped another brief tornado just southeast of Odessa but no significant damage was reported.Another severe thunderstorm, which began in Smith county, Kansas, moved just north of the stateline and produced at least two confirmed touchdowns.  One was reported two miles north of Bostwick but did little damage.  Another tornado passed just to the southeast of Chester and damaged a farmstead.  The tornado sat down about a mile east of town then travelled southeast.  Damage at the farm was mainly confined to a couple of outbuildings.  The people in the farm house were not injured.  At least 25 center irrigation pivots were lost to the storm.  Damaging baseball sized hail was noted in Nelson and near Red Cloud.  Hail to the size of soft balls was reported eight miles southeast of Red Cloud.  In Clay county, residents were nailed twice with strong winds and hail during the evening.  The first occurred near 6 pm as 60 to 80 mph winds roared across the county.  The winds were likely outflow from the severe thunderstorm just to the south of the area.  At least two farmsteads had out building damage and tree damage was significant.  About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three foot diameter tree uprooted.  Four to five inch diameter limbs off other trees.  Pacific Springs area.
2 funnel clouds
3/4 inch hail accumulated to a depth of several inches.
Around 5 inches of rain fell between 1800 and 2000 MST.  Many homes had basements flooded with water and mud.  Several cars were stalled.  Roads were damaged. Waters started receding around 2100 MST,
A short lived-weak tornado occurred 15 miles west of Kimball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unusual post-Memorial Day frost and freeze left its mark on crops across a small part of south-central Nebraska.  The most concentrated area of damaged fields stretched along the Republican River from Red Cloud to Cambridge.  Nearly every community along the river reported overnight low temperatures for the 4th and 5th near 32 degrees.  Low lying areas near the river's edge dropped into the upper 20s under the clear sky.  The result was damaged leaves on several thousand acres of corn and soybeans.  Fortunately, in most cases, the growing point of the plants was not damaged enough to stop plant growth.  After the frost, warmer and wet weather provided nearly ideal conditions for the crops to rebound to normal with very little, if any, yield loss expected.

Severe thunderstorms roared across south-central Nebraska just after midnight CDT downing power lines, damaging buildings and over turning irrigation center pivots.  The storms slipped southeast through Bertrand at 12:45 am CDT knocking power out to a portion of the town.  As the thunderstorms moved southeast, they intensified and eventually produced 60 to 80 mph winds in a 20 mile wide swath from 20 miles southwest of Hastings to 30 miles southeast of York.The damage began near Bladen as 20 pivots were upset in northern Webster county.  The most extensive damage was reported from southern Clay and northern Thayer counties.  In Glenvil, large trees were uprooted and grain bins blown apart.  At Carlton, eight large grain bins were destroyed and a grain dryer overturned.  In Davenport, windows were blown out of homes by the wind.  Some homes and most out buildings in the high wind corridor sustained minor damage as well.  In all it was estimated 75 to 100 center pivots were overturned by the wind.  Portions of northern Thayer county were without power for 12 hours.

After weeks of unseasonably warm weather across south-central Nebraska, a strong arctic surge brought a quick end to the warmth.  Temperatures steadily declined into the single digits by the evening of the 20th as arctic air sagged south across the area.  Snowfall for the event was light and ranged from 1 to 3 inches.  As the cold air deepened by the morning of the 22nd, temperatures fell well below zero.  The coldest morning of the season so far was on the 23rd, when temperatures dropped as low as 17 below zero with clear skies.  Wind chill readings ranged from 20 to 45 below zero.  The cold surge gave way to warmer weather by Christmas Day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs blown down.

Two clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across south-central Nebraska in the evening and early morning hours.  The first dumped hail as large as soft balls on Holdrege and the surrounding area.  Dozens of cars and trucks were damaged or totaled by the large hail.  A fertilizer operation southwest of town sustained major damage to a storage bin as the hail went through the steel roof.  Roofs and siding was damaged along with windows broken and blown in.  The first round of thunderstorms also spawned two brief tornado touchdowns to the west of Minden.  Both were reported by trained spotters in the area.  Large hail north of Arapahoe severely damaged the wheat crop in the area.The second round of storms rolled through around midnight CDT with torrential rains and wind driven hail.  Damage was generally confined to crops and, in particular, across a part of northern Hamilton county.  As many as fifty sections of beans, corn, and alfalfa were damaged by the wind and hail.  Nine sections sustained major damage.  Throughout the area, widespread one to four inch rains posed problems for low lying areas and waterlogged early season crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down and traveled for two miles in open country and did no damage.
Large tree branches blown down.
Extensive tree damage due to thunderstorm winds.  Tree blown onto roof of house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A devastating thunderstorm packing hail and high winds hammered the town of Elm Creek shortly before 6 pm. The severe storm dumped 1.75 inch hail driven by 100 mph winds.  Nearly every home in town sustained some damage in the form on broken windows, damaged siding or extensive tree damage.  Two semi-trailer trucks were blown off Interstate 80.  One person received minor injuries there.  Many windshields were taken by the storm and grain bins were also damaged Some people estimated the hail and wind lasted for 12 minutes.  Three other minor injuries were reported due to flying glass.Another storm deposited dime size hail in Dannebrog at 615 pm.  Just a short time later, this storm blew out some windows north of town.  Minor crop damage was also found in Howard county.

Severe Thunderstorms rumbled across much of south-central Nebraska through the evening hours.  A brief tornado passed northwest of Gilead damaging a shed, irrigation pipe and some fence.  The majority of reports were large hail.  The hail knocked out a windshield at Waco while thunderstorms covered the ground with large hail just south of Gilead.  Given the time of year, crop damage was held to a minimum.  Property damage was also minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 6  inches accompanied by winds 30-40 mph whipped up drifts 2 to 4 feet.

Late evening severe thunderstorms rolled across the northeast half of south-central Nebraska dropping hail and heavy rains with their passage.  The thunderstorms moved southeast across Howard and Merrick counties near 10 pm and dumped hail the size of softballs near St. Paul and Palmer.  Windshields were damaged and windows knocked out of homes near Palmer.  Later, thunderstorms in Clay county dumped large hail and overturned a pivot system southeast of Saronville.  Significant crop damage was reported in southeast Valley county and across Howard and Merrick counties.  Much of the alfalfa crop was leveled as was much of the corn and soybeans in the path of the thunderstorms.  Many farmers replanted their crops.  In some places, drifts of hail were still in fields and lawns 24 hours after the thunderstorms passed.

Thunderstorms rumbling across south-central Nebraska early in the day were responsible for dropping large hail and heavy rain in parts of the area.  Hail the size of baseballs was reported.  Windshields were shattered in the Lexington area and torrential rainfall forced water over US Highway 30 for a short time west of Kearney.  The heavy rains caused the roof over the Kearney/Buffalo County law enforcement building to collapse.  Damage to the 911 center was estimated to be near $400,000.  Street flooding was common in the rest of Kearney.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm produced baseball size hail 6 miles northeast of Gurley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Midday thunderstorms spawned a tornado south of York damaging a farmstead significantly.  The tornado set down two miles north of Fairmont and was on the ground for seven miles before lifting just east of McCool Junction.  The tornado nearly destroyed at least one farmstead and caused damage to power poles and lines and center pivot systems.  A semi tractor trailer was overturned three miles southeast of York.  The driver sustained minor injuries.  The same storm also produced a brief tornado south of Benedict destroying two hog confinement facilities but no hogs were injured.

Widespread thunderstorm activity across southern Nebraska dumped heavy rain, hail, and produced strong winds.  Wind gusts from 55 to 80 mph were common throughout the area with some tree damage northwest of Stromsburg, near Scotia, and six inch limbs broken in Trumbull.  Hail to 1.75 inch diameter resulted in sporadic, but minor, crop damage.

Severe Thunderstorms ripped across south-central Nebraska dropping large hail, very heavy rains and producing wind gusts to 70 mph.  South of Ravenna, several hog sheds on a farmstead were destroyed by high winds.  Corn and soybean crops in the area were heavily damaged.  The swath of destructive hail moved northeast across northwest Howard, northeast Sherman, southeast Valley and the southern half of Greeley counties.  In this area, over 400,000 acres of alfalfa, corn and soybeans were damaged.  Up to 70 percent damage was reported in Greeley county.  Near Elba, hail and wind broke windows and damaged homes.  In addition to the hail and wind, heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches was reported near Scotia.  Widespread 1 to 4 inch rains were reported elsewhere.

Trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds along county roads 10 miles northeast of Scottsbluff.
A tornado briefly touched down over open country 20 miles southwest of Chadron.
A storm produced quarter size hail 15 miles west  of Hemmingford.
Up to 4 inches of rain fell from thunderstorms 20 miles southwest of Chadron, which caused local flooding of streams in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late evening severe thunderstorms rolled across the northeast half of south-central Nebraska dropping hail and heavy rains with their passage.  The thunderstorms moved southeast across Howard and Merrick counties near 10 pm and dumped hail the size of softballs near St. Paul and Palmer.  Windshields were damaged and windows knocked out of homes near Palmer.  Later, thunderstorms in Clay county dumped large hail and overturned a pivot system southeast of Saronville.  Significant crop damage was reported in southeast Valley county and across Howard and Merrick counties.  Much of the alfalfa crop was leveled as was much of the corn and soybeans in the path of the thunderstorms.  Many farmers replanted their crops.  In some places, drifts of hail were still in fields and lawns 24 hours after the thunderstorms passed.

After weeks of unseasonably warm weather across south-central Nebraska, a strong arctic surge brought a quick end to the warmth.  Temperatures steadily declined into the single digits by the evening of the 20th as arctic air sagged south across the area.  Snowfall for the event was light and ranged from 1 to 3 inches.  As the cold air deepened by the morning of the 22nd, temperatures fell well below zero.  The coldest morning of the season so far was on the 23rd, when temperatures dropped as low as 17 below zero with clear skies.  Wind chill readings ranged from 20 to 45 below zero.  The cold surge gave way to warmer weather by Christmas Day.

Severe thunderstorms roared across south-central Nebraska just after midnight CDT downing power lines, damaging buildings and over turning irrigation center pivots.  The storms slipped southeast through Bertrand at 12:45 am CDT knocking power out to a portion of the town.  As the thunderstorms moved southeast, they intensified and eventually produced 60 to 80 mph winds in a 20 mile wide swath from 20 miles southwest of Hastings to 30 miles southeast of York.The damage began near Bladen as 20 pivots were upset in northern Webster county.  The most extensive damage was reported from southern Clay and northern Thayer counties.  In Glenvil, large trees were uprooted and grain bins blown apart.  At Carlton, eight large grain bins were destroyed and a grain dryer overturned.  In Davenport, windows were blown out of homes by the wind.  Some homes and most out buildings in the high wind corridor sustained minor damage as well.  In all it was estimated 75 to 100 center pivots were overturned by the wind.  Portions of northern Thayer county were without power for 12 hours.

A devastating thunderstorm packing hail and high winds hammered the town of Elm Creek shortly before 6 pm. The severe storm dumped 1.75 inch hail driven by 100 mph winds.  Nearly every home in town sustained some damage in the form on broken windows, damaged siding or extensive tree damage.  Two semi-trailer trucks were blown off Interstate 80.  One person received minor injuries there.  Many windshields were taken by the storm and grain bins were also damaged Some people estimated the hail and wind lasted for 12 minutes.  Three other minor injuries were reported due to flying glass.Another storm deposited dime size hail in Dannebrog at 615 pm.  Just a short time later, this storm blew out some windows north of town.  Minor crop damage was also found in Howard county.

Severe Thunderstorms rumbled across much of south-central Nebraska through the evening hours.  A brief tornado passed northwest of Gilead damaging a shed, irrigation pipe and some fence.  The majority of reports were large hail.  The hail knocked out a windshield at Waco while thunderstorms covered the ground with large hail just south of Gilead.  Given the time of year, crop damage was held to a minimum.  Property damage was also minor.

Severe Thunderstorms ripped across south-central Nebraska dropping large hail, very heavy rains and producing wind gusts to 70 mph.  South of Ravenna, several hog sheds on a farmstead were destroyed by high winds.  Corn and soybean crops in the area were heavily damaged.  The swath of destructive hail moved northeast across northwest Howard, northeast Sherman, southeast Valley and the southern half of Greeley counties.  In this area, over 400,000 acres of alfalfa, corn and soybeans were damaged.  Up to 70 percent damage was reported in Greeley county.  Near Elba, hail and wind broke windows and damaged homes.  In addition to the hail and wind, heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches was reported near Scotia.  Widespread 1 to 4 inch rains were reported elsewhere.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced up to 2 inches of rain in the Kimball area, resulting in the shutting down of  the underpass of Highway 71 and the Union Pacific tracks.  A few local businesses received damage as well.
Thunderstorms produced 3 inches of rain across Cheyenne County, which caused the flooding of two county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Thunderstorms rumbled across much of south-central Nebraska through the evening hours.  A brief tornado passed northwest of Gilead damaging a shed, irrigation pipe and some fence.  The majority of reports were large hail. Property damage was also minor.

A very unusual post-Memorial Day frost and freeze left its mark on crops across a small part of south-central Nebraska.  The most concentrated area of damaged fields stretched along the Republican River from Red Cloud to Cambridge.  Nearly every community along the river reported overnight low temperatures for the 4th and 5th near 32 degrees.  Low lying areas near the river's edge dropped into the upper 20s under the clear sky.  The result was damaged leaves on several thousand acres of corn and soybeans.  Fortunately, in most cases, the growing point of the plants was not damaged enough to stop plant growth.  After the frost, warmer and wet weather provided nearly ideal conditions for the crops to rebound to normal with very little, if any, yield loss expected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across south-central Nebraska in the evening and early morning hours.  The first dumped hail as large as soft balls on Holdrege and the surrounding area.  Dozens of cars and trucks were damaged or totaled by the large hail.  A fertilizer operation southwest of town sustained major damage to a storage bin as the hail went through the steel roof.  Roofs and siding was damaged along with windows broken and blown in.  The first round of thunderstorms also spawned two brief tornado touchdowns to the west of Minden.  Both were reported by trained spotters in the area.  Large hail north of Arapahoe severely damaged the wheat crop in the area.The second round of storms rolled through around midnight CDT with torrential rains and wind driven hail.  Damage was generally confined to crops and, in particular, across a part of northern Hamilton county.  As many as fifty sections of beans, corn, and alfalfa were damaged by the wind and hail.  Nine sections sustained major damage.  Throughout the area, widespread one to four inch rains posed problems for low lying areas and waterlogged early season crops.

Very strong northwest winds gusting over 60 mph belted south-central Nebraska for most of the day.  Sustained wind speeds from 35 to 45 mph were common all day long with gusts over 60 mph reported.  Ord reported a wind gusts of 67 mph for the highest gust in the area.  Aurora and Grand Island reported wind gusts to 61 and 60 mph respectively. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm affected the Nebraska panhandle, with 12 inches of snowfall reported in Broadwater,  A total of 10 inches of  snow fell in Kimball and Chadron, with 8 inches of total snowfall in Potter and across Dawes county.  Up to 7 inches of snow was reported across the rest of the panhandle.  Highways in the central and northern panhandle were icy and snow packed.  Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times in Scottsbluff and in Chadron.  A man lost control of his vehicle on icy Interstate 80, 3 miles west of Lodgepole.  The vehicle rolled onto it's top and slid into a ditch and was totalled.  The driver of the vehicle was not injured. Another rollover accident occurred one mile east of Lodgepole.  A driver rolled his vehicle into a ditch while trying to change lanes on the icy Interstate 80.  Two passengers suffered head injuries.  A driver lost control of his vehicle on Highway 30, 4 miles west of Lodgepole.  The car rolled but the driver was not injured.A driver of a semi-truck collided with a snowplow, 9 miles east of Sidney, due to icy roads and poor visibilities.  The collision caused $1000 damage to the snowplow and 5,000 to the semi.Another accident occurred 9 miles east of Sidney on Interstate 80, in which a semi-truck jackknifed.  The jackknifed semi rolled and the rear trailer broke open.  The driver was taken to the hospital for head injuries and a passenger was treated and released form the hospital for neck and back injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm produced golf ball size hail 20 miles SW of Agate or 5 miles NE of Henry.
Golf ball size hail fell 8 miles north of Lodgepole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm dumped up to eight inches of snow in the Northeast Nebraska Panhandle.  Snow and blowing snow made travel almost impossible so that roads were closed and school was canceled.  High winds in excess of 60 mph blew a metal utility building into cars in a nearby parking lot in Broken Bow.  The winds flipped a metal building into a motel in Valentine.  The destructive winds also lifted and tore off roof shingles, damaged a garage door and tore siding off of a country school south of North Platte.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy upstream rains caused flooding along the Shell Creek.  Some damage was reported in the Lindsay area.
Lightning struck a home causing a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure which tracked across eastern Nebraska brought a long period of very strong west to northwest winds to the area.  The barometric pressure from this storm dropped to 28.86 inches at Omaha Eppley-Airfield at 225 am cst...the 5th lowest on record.  Wind gusts of 45 mph to around 65 mph were common over all of eastern Nebraska from around 200 am cst through 400 pm cst before subsiding.  The high winds toppled a few weaker trees, downed or snapped some power poles in rural areas, overturned several semi-tractor trailer trucks and did isolated sign damage.   Peak wind gusts and time of occurrence included...66 mph at the Valley NWS office at 1110 am...63 mph at Fremont at 210 pm...61 mph at Tekamah at 530 am...58 mph at Offutt AFB at 334 pm...55 mph at Omaha Eppley-Airfield at 112 pm...55 mph at Columbus at 534 am...54 mph at Beatrice at 225 pm...52 mph at Falls City at 1152 am and 1252 pm and 48 mph at Lincoln at 220 am and 1244 pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground.

A warm front lifting north and a strong jet stream played a key role in the eruption of severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon in south-central Nebraska.  Large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds and two tornadic thunderstorms caused millions of dollars in damage.The first tornadic thunderstorm occurred in northern Gosper county just outside of the town of Elwood.  The tornado sat down to the southwest of Elwood then travelled east.  After a mile, the tornado turned and travelled due north.  It hit one farmstead, heavily damaging the home and totally destroying several outbuildings.  The path of the tornado when right down the middle of US highway 283.  A truck driver and a family of three were travelling north of Elwood when they realized the tornado was heading toward them.  They abandon their vehicles and sought cover in a ditch.  The tornado passed nearly overhead but the four survived with only minor injuries.  The one car was totalled and the semi-truck, loaded with ice cream, was heavily damaged.  The cargo was a total loss.  This same severe thunderstorm marched northeast and dropped another brief tornado just southeast of Odessa but no significant damage was reported.Another severe thunderstorm, which began in Smith county, Kansas, moved just north of the stateline and produced at least two confirmed touchdowns.  One was reported two miles north of Bostwick but did little damage.  Another tornado passed just to the southeast of Chester and damaged a farmstead.  The tornado sat down about a mile east of town then travelled southeast.  Damage at the farm was mainly confined to a couple of outbuildings.  The people in the farm house were not injured.  At least 25 center irrigation pivots were lost to the storm.  Damaging baseball sized hail was noted in Nelson and near Red Cloud.  Hail to the size of soft balls was reported eight miles southeast of Red Cloud.  In Clay county, residents were nailed twice with strong winds and hail during the evening.  The first occurred near 6 pm as 60 to 80 mph winds roared across the county.  The winds were likely outflow from the severe thunderstorm just to the south of the area.  At least two farmsteads had out building damage and tree damage was significant.  About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm produced a wind gust of 80 mph 9 miles north of Dix, which damaged a roof of a home.
A storm produced golfball size hail in Whitney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding.  Sewer systems in the Fremont area backed up into basements of homes causing damage.  York Creek near Herman and Bell Creek just east of Arlington overflowed their banks.  Some homes east of Arlington had to be evacuated.  Eight of these homes were flooded.  A portion of U.S. highway 30 near Arlington had to be closed for a period of time.  Nearly 3000 acres of soybeans were destroyed near Arlington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees downed.
Windows blown out of pumper truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense winter storm dumped up to eight inches of snow in the Northeast Nebraska Panhandle.  Snow and blowing snow made travel almost impossible so that roads were closed and school was canceled.  High winds in excess of 60 mph blew a metal utility building into cars in a nearby parking lot in Broken Bow.  The winds flipped a metal building into a motel in Valentine.  The destructive winds also lifted and tore off roof shingles, damaged a garage door and tore siding off of a country school south of North Platte.

A major winter storm moved through the central plains and created near blizzard conditions over portions of eastern Nebraska.  Heavy snow combined with strong northerly winds of 40-45 mph created considerable blowing snow with 6-15 foot snow drifts common.  The heaviest snow fell in an area from Gage county northeast through the south and southeast portion of the Omaha metropolitan area where 11-16 inches of snow fell. Lesser amounts occurred further to the north with 4-6 inches at Norfolk.  Schools and businesses were closed for a few days as the strong winds continued to cause blowing snow making the task of cleaning up very difficult.

A major winter storm moved through the central plains and created near blizzard conditions over portions of eastern Nebraska.  Heavy snow combined with strong northerly winds of 40-45 mph created considerable blowing snow with 6-15 foot snow drifts common.  The heaviest snow fell in an area from Gage county northeast through the south and southeast portion of the Omaha metropolitan area where 11-16 inches of snow fell. Lesser amounts occurred further to the north with 4-6 inches at Norfolk.  Schools and businesses were closed for a few days as the strong winds continued to cause blowing snow making the task of cleaning up very difficult.

Severe thunderstorms roared across south-central Nebraska just after midnight CDT downing power lines, damaging buildings and over turning irrigation center pivots.  The storms slipped southeast through Bertrand at 12:45 am CDT knocking power out to a portion of the town.  As the thunderstorms moved southeast, they intensified and eventually produced 60 to 80 mph winds in a 20 mile wide swath from 20 miles southwest of Hastings to 30 miles southeast of York.The damage began near Bladen as 20 pivots were upset in northern Webster county.  The most extensive damage was reported from southern Clay and northern Thayer counties.  In Glenvil, large trees were uprooted and grain bins blown apart.  At Carlton, eight large grain bins were destroyed and a grain dryer overturned.  In Davenport, windows were blown out of homes by the wind.  Some homes and most out buildings in the high wind corridor sustained minor damage as well.  In all it was estimated 75 to 100 center pivots were overturned by the wind.  Portions of northern Thayer county were without power for 12 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs downed.
Numerous power lines downed.
Large tree blown down.
Golfball hail damaged buildings and destroyed crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting north and a strong jet stream played a key role in the eruption of severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon in south-central Nebraska.  Large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds and two tornadic thunderstorms caused millions of dollars in damage.The first tornadic thunderstorm occurred in northern Gosper county just outside of the town of Elwood.  The tornado sat down to the southwest of Elwood then travelled east.  After a mile, the tornado turned and travelled due north.  It hit one farmstead, heavily damaging the home and totally destroying several outbuildings.  The path of the tornado when right down the middle of US highway 283.  A truck driver and a family of three were travelling north of Elwood when they realized the tornado was heading toward them.  They abandon their vehicles and sought cover in a ditch.  The tornado passed nearly overhead but the four survived with only minor injuries.  The one car was totalled and the semi-truck, loaded with ice cream, was heavily damaged.  The cargo was a total loss.  This same severe thunderstorm marched northeast and dropped another brief tornado just southeast of Odessa but no significant damage was reported.Another severe thunderstorm, which began in Smith county, Kansas, moved just north of the stateline and produced at least two confirmed touchdowns.  One was reported two miles north of Bostwick but did little damage.  Another tornado passed just to the southeast of Chester and damaged a farmstead.  The tornado sat down about a mile east of town then travelled southeast.  Damage at the farm was mainly confined to a couple of outbuildings.  The people in the farm house were not injured.  At least 25 center irrigation pivots were lost to the storm.  Damaging baseball sized hail was noted in Nelson and near Red Cloud.  Hail to the size of soft balls was reported eight miles southeast of Red Cloud.  In Clay county, residents were nailed twice with strong winds and hail during the evening.  The first occurred near 6 pm as 60 to 80 mph winds roared across the county.  The winds were likely outflow from the severe thunderstorm just to the south of the area.  At least two farmsteads had out building damage and tree damage was significant.  About three hours later, a different severe thunderstorm dumped golf ball sized hail while producing 60+ mph winds in central Clay county.  Many vehicles in Clay Center were damaged and 25 windows were shattered in the Courthouse.  Corn and soybean loss totalled over two million dollars as some crops were levelled by the wind and hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One grain bin destroyed and another damaged.
50 X 80 foot honey storage barn on the west side of Randolph was destroyed.  Roof on the Randolph High School was damaged.
High winds caused some damage to farms.  Trees downed.
Hog confinement building destroyed.
A storm produced golf ball size hail 3 miles west of Whitney.
A tornado touched down 12 miles west of  Kimball over open country and quickly dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After weeks of unseasonably warm weather across south-central Nebraska, a strong arctic surge brought a quick end to the warmth.  Temperatures steadily declined into the single digits by the evening of the 20th as arctic air sagged south across the area.  Snowfall for the event was light and ranged from 1 to 3 inches.  As the cold air deepened by the morning of the 22nd, temperatures fell well below zero.  The coldest morning of the season so far was on the 23rd, when temperatures dropped as low as 17 below zero with clear skies.  Wind chill readings ranged from 20 to 45 below zero.  The cold surge gave way to warmer weather by Christmas Day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home causing a fire.

Thunderstorm produced 5 to 7 inches of rain along the Turkey Creek just west of Wilber resulting in extensive agricultural damage and a number of secondary road closures.  Some roads, bridges, and culverts were damaged.
Lightning struck a tree.
Large tree downed.
A storm produced  quarter size hail 9 miles south of Chadron.
Trees and power lines downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Thunderstorms ripped across south-central Nebraska dropping large hail, very heavy rains and producing wind gusts to 70 mph.  South of Ravenna, several hog sheds on a farmstead were destroyed by high winds.  Corn and soybean crops in the area were heavily damaged.  The swath of destructive hail moved northeast across northwest Howard, northeast Sherman, southeast Valley and the southern half of Greeley counties.  In this area, over 400,000 acres of alfalfa, corn and soybeans were damaged.  Up to 70 percent damage was reported in Greeley county.  Near Elba, hail and wind broke windows and damaged homes.  In addition to the hail and wind, heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches was reported near Scotia.  Widespread 1 to 4 inch rains were reported elsewhere.

In addition to the hail and wind, heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches was reported near Scotia.  Widespread 1 to 4 inch rains were reported elsewhere.

Two clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms erupted across south-central Nebraska in the evening and early morning hours.  The first dumped hail as large as soft balls on Holdrege and the surrounding area.  Dozens of cars and trucks were damaged or totaled by the large hail.  A fertilizer operation southwest of town sustained major damage to a storage bin as the hail went through the steel roof.  Roofs and siding was damaged along with windows broken and blown in.  The first round of thunderstorms also spawned two brief tornado touchdowns to the west of Minden.  Both were reported by trained spotters in the area.  Large hail north of Arapahoe severely damaged the wheat crop in the area.The second round of storms rolled through around midnight CDT with torrential rains and wind driven hail.  Damage was generally confined to crops and, in particular, across a part of northern Hamilton county.  As many as fifty sections of beans, corn, and alfalfa were damaged by the wind and hail.  Nine sections sustained major damage.  Throughout the area, widespread one to four inch rains posed problems for low lying areas and waterlogged early season crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches downed.
Many large tree limbs downed.  Considerable tree damage.  Some windows blown out.
Trees and large branches downed.
Large tree branches downed at 27th St. and Highway 2.
Power outages and tree limbs downed in Beatrice.
Grain bin moved.  Tree damaged.  Metal roofing torn off shed.
Center pivot systems toppled and damaged.
Window broken out of office furniture store near downtown.  Severe damage occurred to merchandise near the window.

A devastating thunderstorm packing hail and high winds hammered the town of Elm Creek shortly before 6 pm. The severe storm dumped 1.75 inch hail driven by 100 mph winds.  Nearly every home in town sustained some damage in the form on broken windows, damaged siding or extensive tree damage.  Two semi-trailer trucks were blown off Interstate 80.  One person received minor injuries there.  Many windshields were taken by the storm and grain bins were also damaged Some people estimated the hail and wind lasted for 12 minutes.  Three other minor injuries were reported due to flying glass.Another storm deposited dime size hail in Dannebrog at 615 pm.  Just a short time later, this storm blew out some windows north of town.  Minor crop damage was also found in Howard county.

A very unusual post-Memorial Day frost and freeze left its mark on crops across a small part of south-central Nebraska.  The most concentrated area of damaged fields stretched along the Republican River from Red Cloud to Cambridge.  Nearly every community along the river reported overnight low temperatures for the 4th and 5th near 32 degrees.  Low lying areas near the river's edge dropped into the upper 20s under the clear sky.  The result was damaged leaves on several thousand acres of corn and soybeans.  Fortunately, in most cases, the growing point of the plants was not damaged enough to stop plant growth.  After the frost, warmer and wet weather provided nearly ideal conditions for the crops to rebound to normal with very little, if any, yield loss expected.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck 911 transmitter forcing sheriffs department to use cell phones for 20 minutes.
Minor damage to trees and a few cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure which tracked across eastern Nebraska brought a long period of very strong west to northwest winds to the area.  The barometric pressure from this storm dropped to 28.86 inches at Omaha Eppley-Airfield at 225 am cst...the 5th lowest on record.  Wind gusts of 45 mph to around 65 mph were common over all of eastern Nebraska from around 200 am cst through 400 pm cst before subsiding.  The high winds toppled a few weaker trees, downed or snapped some power poles in rural areas, overturned several semi-tractor trailer trucks and did isolated sign damage.   Peak wind gusts and time of occurrence included...66 mph at the Valley NWS office at 1110 am...63 mph at Fremont at 210 pm...61 mph at Tekamah at 530 am...58 mph at Offutt AFB at 334 pm...55 mph at Omaha Eppley-Airfield at 112 pm...55 mph at Columbus at 534 am...54 mph at Beatrice at 225 pm...52 mph at Falls City at 1152 am and 1252 pm and 48 mph at Lincoln at 220 am and 1244 pm.

Thunderstorms rumbling across south-central Nebraska early in the day were responsible for dropping large hail and heavy rain in parts of the area.  Hail the size of baseballs was reported.  Windshields were shattered in the Lexington area and torrential rainfall forced water over US Highway 30 for a short time west of Kearney.  The heavy rains caused the roof over the Kearney/Buffalo County law enforcement building to collapse.  Damage to the 911 center was estimated to be near $400,000.  Street flooding was common in the rest of Kearney.

One person received minor injuries there.  Many windshields were taken by the storm and grain bins were also damaged Some people estimated the hail and wind lasted for 12 minutes.  Three other minor injuries were reported due to flying glass.Another storm deposited dime size hail in Dannebrog at 615 pm.  Just a short time later, this storm blew out some windows north of town.  Minor crop damage was also found in Howard county.

Scattered thunderstorms pushed southeast across south-central Nebraska during the late evening hours.  Strong winds and some hail accompanied the storms.  A large tree limb fell on a roof in Hastings causing some minor damage.  In Kenesaw, one and one-half inches of rain fell in less than 40 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds caused porch to collapse at one home and damaged a deck on a mobile home. Numerous 1" diameter branches broken in Benkelman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Thunderstorms rumbled across much of south-central Nebraska through the evening hours.  A brief tornado passed northwest of Gilead damaging a shed, irrigation pipe and some fence.  The majority of reports were large hail.  The hail knocked out a windshield at Waco while thunderstorms covered the ground with large hail just south of Gilead.  Given the time of year, crop damage was held to a minimum.  Property damage was also minor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a windmill near a home and followed the wiring into a nearby house, sparking a fire.  Damage was minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the chimney of a home causing damage.
Thunderstorms produced strong winds which destroyed several outbuildings just northwest of Eagle Field.
Strong thunderstorm winds rolled a trailer home causing damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a gust of 80 mph 11 miles southeast of Redington.
A storm produced wind gusts up to 60 mph 8 miles southeast of Broadwater.
Stationary thunderstorms produced up to 2.5 inches of rain across Kimball County.  Water was flowing over roads in northern Kimball County.
A tornado briefly touched down over open country 15 miles southwest of Bridgeport.
Thunderstorm winds caused extensive tree damage in the community of Mullen.
Hail broke windows and damaged roof of house. Extensive crop damage.
Hail accompanied by 45 to 50 mph winds.  Damage to cars, campers,  and mobile homes.

Wind damage to pole barns
Strong winds rolled a trailer house.
Metal outbuilding pulled out of ground and thrown 30 feet.

Half dollar size hail heavily damaged corn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms roared across south-central Nebraska just after midnight CDT downing power lines, damaging buildings and over turning irrigation center pivots.  The storms slipped southeast through Bertrand at 12:45 am CDT knocking power out to a portion of the town.  As the thunderstorms moved southeast, they intensified and eventually produced 60 to 80 mph winds in a 20 mile wide swath from 20 miles southwest of Hastings to 30 miles southeast of York.The damage began near Bladen as 20 pivots were upset in northern Webster county.  The most extensive damage was reported from southern Clay and northern Thayer counties.  In Glenvil, large trees were uprooted and grain bins blown apart.  At Carlton, eight large grain bins were destroyed and a grain dryer overturned.  In Davenport, windows were blown out of homes by the wind.  Some homes and most out buildings in the high wind corridor sustained minor damage as well.  In all it was estimated 75 to 100 center pivots were overturned by the wind.  Portions of northern Thayer county were without power for 12 hours.
